story:
I planned my “perfect last day” the way people do for a beloved old dog—twenty-two treats for my twenty-two years. I did my makeup, dropped off my cat, Sebastian, with Nathan, the nicest friend I’d made at school, and spent the day feeding myself: breakfast, lunch, dinner, plus licorice and Haribo peaches from an overpriced candy store. I took myself to the Botanic Garden, tore a blueberry muffin apart at a metal café table, and watched the pond go still. By the time I staggered out of Harold’s Steakhouse at twilight, full to the pores, summer in Chicago was humming. Traffic idled on Halsted; voices braided from patios and car windows into one big, incoherent song. Usually the city made me feel invisible; that night I pressed my back to the steakhouse’s brick and watched, trying to memorize what I’d never see again. The grand finale on my list was to sneak into a concert. It was Friday; there had to be music somewhere.

I headed south, letting rhythms tug me along—the scrape of my sandals, snatches of radios, laughter becoming melody. While I stared at a teenager chaining his bike with a ridiculous lock, I tripped over a girl sitting on the sidewalk with a spread of cards. I scraped my toes, scuffed my new sandals, and dropped to my knees to help. She smiled, freckles like constellations, gold earrings swaying. “Maybe it’s a sign,” she said. “You want a reading?” They weren’t playing cards but Tarot. Her name was Hazel.

Under a streetlight, for ten dollars, she shuffled. A card fell. “Leave it,” she said. The Fool, reversed. Then another slipped free: The Wheel of Fortune. “The Fool upside down,” she told me softly, “is acting without thinking about consequences—living only in the moment. The Wheel says life turns. Every low gives way to a high.” Heat crept under my skin. It felt less like a trick and more like being seen.

Hazel asked if I wanted a drink and promised live music. We skipped—literally—through the warm dark to a tiny bar I would’ve missed, smoky with the ghosts of cigarettes and a skunky sweetness. Jazz wrapped around us. I wasn’t a beer girl, but I drank. She asked what had brought me to her. I dodged the question and showed her a text from Nathan: a photo of Sebastian with “Sebastian misses you.” She lit up and asked if Nathan was my boyfriend. No, I said. Then she said, “I bet he can’t wait for you to come back,” and the words undid me. I cried—I’m not a public crier—and she paid and tugged me outside.

We walked into a cooler breeze. I thought of the wheel and how heat breaks. At a crosswalk, she said, “Do you want to know why I’m named Hazel?” She told me her mother had once planned to end her life. On the train, an old woman with extraordinary hazel eyes grabbed her mother’s wrist and said, “Don’t do it.” Her mother didn’t. She named her daughter after those eyes.

We had wandered to Nathan’s building. The motion light bloomed on. Hazel’s eyes were the exact hazel you don’t forget. “Go get your cat,” she said, then hugged me hard; I felt her slip something into my pocket. “Don’t do it,” she whispered, before skipping away. I stood there until the city went quiet enough to hear my breath. It was 12:05—a new day. In my pocket were The Fool and The Wheel.

I called upstairs. “Hey,” I said. “I’m here for Sebastian.”
